lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market advertising agencies that create campaigns, creative assets, media plans, campaign operations, and performance reporting for external clients on recurring retainers or repeat engagements.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off internal marketing teams, captive units, pure software vendors, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and agencies without recurring or repeat external-client service revenue.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External business clients, paid through retainers, project fees, media-related fees, commissions, or combinations of those arrangements.

a.rationale: The occupation mix is concentrated in management, arts/design/media, advertising sales, and office roles. Drafting, versioning, research, campaign reporting, workflow coordination, and routine production are substantially AI-exposed; client persuasion, strategy, taste, negotiation, and final accountability remain materially human. The estimate weights the higher-paid management and specialist work more heavily than headcount alone.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-linked NAICS 541810 occupation file, time-and-motion studies from advertising agencies, and client-level before-and-after staffing records for creative, media, and account workflows.
caveats: OEWS is for broader NAICS 541800, excludes self-employed workers, and is not a task-exposure measure.
No source reports a wage-weighted task-substitution measure for the frozen NAICS 541810 lens.

rho.bridge: The 4As survey measures respondent agencies' current GenAI use and stated operating barriers, not the five-year implemented share of exposed labor opportunity for lower-middle-market NAICS 541810 firms. I translate broad adoption, limited work-share use, and reported legal, quality, skill, and business-model barriers into a bounded five-year operational implementation judgment; the survey's likely larger and more sophisticated agency respondents may bias implementation upward for the lens.
rho.rationale: Agency adoption is already widespread, but use remains shallow for much of the work and legal, privacy, reliability, and talent constraints limit unattended deployment. Five years should permit workflow redesign beyond experimentation, while preserving human review for brand, client, and platform-risk decisions.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal 4As or ANA agency panel reporting labor hours by workflow before and after deployment, plus audited vendor usage logs segmented by agency revenue and employee count.
caveats: The surveys are not a representative NAICS 541810 lower-middle-market sample.
Reported use and intentions are not verified reductions in required labor.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition centers on creating advertising campaigns and placing advertising for clients, which is generally an external service. Eligibility is below one because the code does not identify recurring revenue, repeated-client economics, firm-level EBITDA, or control-transfer suitability.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level Dun & Bradstreet or PrivCo revenue descriptions, agency billing-system data, and a buyer-quality-of-earnings sample that identifies retained clients and normalized EBITDA.
caveats: NAICS classification does not disclose client recurrence, ownership, profitability, or revenue concentration.
The estimate may overstate eligibility where campaign work is sporadic or depends on a founder's personal relationships.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures plans among U.S. business owners, including all employer-firm sizes and industries, rather than completed qualifying control transfers among eligible lower-middle-market advertising agencies. I use its employer-firm five-year sell-or-transfer response as an anchor, lower it for plans that may not close or may not meet the control-transfer definition, and allow an upper case for more transferable established agencies; general employer-firm figures may bias either direction because advertising-agency client concentration and founder dependence are unmeasured.
s5.rationale: Employer-business owners report meaningful five-year transfer intentions, while a substantial portion of owners report closure or uncertainty. The range excludes closures without transferable operations and does not treat internal reorganizations as transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal data from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, or business-broker CRM records tagged to NAICS 541810, together with owner-age and succession-plan data for agencies in the EBITDA band.
caveats: The available evidence is cross-industry and does not isolate the EBITDA band or NAICS 541810.

q.bridge: The 4As survey measures agency views about GenAI remuneration and anticipated business-model change, rather than discounted five-year retention of implemented gross benefit. I use the reported treatment of GenAI as a cost of business and expectations of pricing-model change as evidence of pass-through and competition; survey responses may overstate future pricing discipline because they are stated expectations rather than contract outcomes.
q.rationale: Retainers and value-based work can preserve some productivity benefit, but competitive bidding, client procurement, scope expansion, and clients' expectations of AI-enabled speed make full retention unlikely. The base assumes material but incomplete pass-through after renewal and repricing.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of agency MSAs, SOWs, renewal proposals, and realized gross-margin cohorts that separately track AI-enabled output, labor hours, fee changes, and client volume.
caveats: Billing practices vary sharply among retainers, project fees, commissions, and media-related compensation.
The proxy does not measure actual customer-sharing or competitive price changes.

d5.bridge: BLS projects output for the broader 541800 advertising, public relations, and related-services industry from 197.3 billion chained 2017 dollars in 2024 to 257.3 billion in 2034, not five-year constant-quality demand for NAICS 541810 alone. I compound that ten-year output path to a five-year ratio and widen it for the NAICS and service-basket mismatch; inclusion of public relations and related services can bias the result in either direction.
d5.rationale: The published broader-industry output path supports moderate real demand growth, but the low case allows client in-sourcing, self-service platforms, and automation-driven substitution to offset that growth for the current agency service basket.
d5.change_evidence: BEA industry output and input-output detail separating advertising agencies, agency revenue panels adjusted for media pass-through, and client marketing-spend surveys broken out by outsourced agency service.
caveats: The BLS line is broader than NAICS 541810 and its output concept is not an exact measure of current service-basket demand.
The conversion from a ten-year projection to the requested horizon assumes a smooth path.

o.rationale: Clients can self-serve media tools and use generative systems for basic content, research, and campaign operations. However, accountable operators should remain needed for positioning, creative judgment, integrated execution, client coordination, brand safety, and performance accountability. The wide range reflects the contested boundary between software-enabled agency work and software replacement.
o.change_evidence: Client surveys that track outsourced-agency spend versus in-house and self-serve platform substitution, plus agency cohort retention data by service line and AI-enabled platform disclosures.
caveats: No source directly measures the operator-required share of the frozen lens's year-five service quantity.
Platform product changes and client in-housing can move this measure quickly.

narrative.executive_view: Advertising agencies combine a labor-heavy specialist workflow with fast AI adoption, but the same tools create client expectations of speed and lower-cost production. The opportunity depends on operational discipline and retaining differentiated client-facing judgment.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate research, briefing, draft copy and assets, ad iteration, reporting, reconciliation, and internal coordination. It is less dependable for client trust, strategic positioning, brand-sensitive approval, negotiation, and accountable campaign decisions.
narrative.value_capture: Agency remuneration is mixed. Retainers and value-oriented relationships can retain part of the productivity gain, while project bidding, scope expansion, and an industry trend to treat GenAI as a cost of business expose gains to repricing.
narrative.firm_availability: The NAICS definition is centered on external client advertising services, but it does not reveal recurrence or normalized EBITDA. General employer-owner evidence supports some transfer activity, yet founder relationships and client concentration can prevent otherwise operational firms from transferring cleanly.
narrative.demand_durability: BLS projects real output growth for the broader advertising, public relations, and related-services line. Demand for accountable agency work should persist where clients need integrated strategy and execution, although basic production and media operations are susceptible to self-service and in-housing.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Evidence is broader than NAICS 541810 for occupation mix and demand, and agency surveys may skew toward larger or more technology-active respondents. Legal, privacy, quality, platform, and brand-safety constraints can slow implementation; client concentration and owner dependence can impair transferability.
narrative.principal_driver: A large share of agency production and coordination work can be redesigned with AI while specialist client service remains valuable.
narrative.principal_weakness: AI-enabled productivity is likely to be competed away or absorbed by clients through repricing, self-service, and broader scope expectations.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition for 541810 Advertising Agencies
sources.supports: Defines advertising agencies as establishments creating advertising campaigns and placing advertising in media.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS industry-specific occupational employment and wage estimates for NAICS 541800
sources.supports: Reports the occupation and wage mix for advertising, public relations, and related services, including management, arts/media, sales, and office occupations.

sources.title: O*NET Advertising and Promotions Managers tasks
sources.supports: Lists core advertising-management work such as preparing materials, editing creative, coordinating media, analyzing results, and managing campaign teams.

sources.title: 4As State of GenAI Adoption 2025
sources.supports: Reports agency GenAI adoption, work-share use, legal and operational barriers, expected business-model change, and remuneration treatment.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports owner age and stated five-year sale or transfer intentions for U.S. employer businesses.

sources.title: BLS Employment and output by industry, 2024–34
sources.supports: Projects employment and chained-dollar output for advertising, public relations, and related services.

sources.title: Basis Technologies 2025 advertising-agency AI survey
sources.supports: Reports agency AI workflow use, leading uses, automation investment intentions, inefficiency, cost, margin, and technology-stack challenges.
